video_title: All-In's Best Ideas Pitch Competition: 4 Investors Present Their Top Trades Live
gist: Four hedge-fund/VC managers pitch single best ideas at All-In's live competition: Aaron Cowen (Serreta) long MGM Resorts as a Barry-Diller-in-play Asian/Dubai casino option; Dan Rasmussen-style power thesis on Talen Energy as a sub-replacement-cost nuclear/gas play on AI power demand; Oleg Nelman (EcoR1) long Actis Oncology radiopharma; Kyle Samani long the GeoNet (GEOD) decentralized RTK crypto token. MGM won both audience and bestie votes.

themes.theme: MGM Resorts — Diller-driven sum-of-the-parts casino option

insights.claim: Aaron Cowen of Serreta Capital is long MGM Resorts, arguing the stock is a triple to $100-150 versus a high-$40s price, driven not by Las Vegas but by hidden Asian and Dubai casino optionality.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cowen
insights.so_what: A catalyst-rich, downside-protected long: even base case is a double, with free options on Japan and Dubai.
insights.quote: So we think the stock is a triple. Bar remember Barry's bidding for the company.

insights.claim: Barry Diller now owns 26% of MGM, has bid $48/share, and MGM is ~80% of his NAV, making him a financial (not strategic) buyer trying to capture the Japan opportunity cheaply, which puts the company in play.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cowen
insights.so_what: Insider concentration plus a live bid sets a hard floor and a takeover-arb dynamic; shares shouldn't be tendered at $48.
insights.quote: He is not a strategic buyer. He is a financial buyer and he's doing it to get rich. So therefore, I think this company is now in play.

insights.claim: MGM's Osaka, Japan casino license (sole prefecture to approve, opening 2030, ~$2B EBITDA at 40% ownership plus management fee) is barely mentioned in company presentations and the market historically only prices such assets ~3 years before opening — which is roughly now.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cowen
insights.so_what: A re-rating catalyst window is opening; Cowen values Japan at ~$50/share on top of ~$60 for Vegas+China.
insights.quote: If you also look at where Osaka is located... from Shanghai, it's shorter than Macau and Singapore... Osaka is great. It's also a first world nation.

insights.claim: MGM is building an MGM/Aria/Bellagio complex in Dubai with 300,000 sq ft of empty space pre-built for gambling; if Dubai legalizes casinos (Wynn opens in Al Marjan ~2 years out), that adds an estimated $40-50/share of free optionality.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cowen
insights.so_what: A nearly free call on Gulf gaming legalization; Wynn's Al Marjan opening could pressure Dubai to allow on-property gaming.
insights.quote: they have snuck in this building 300,000 square ft of cu of space. Well, one day if Dubai decides to legalize gambling, guess where it's going? Right there.

insights.claim: MGM has bought back roughly half its float in six years, a buyback pace Cowen says he has rarely seen, alongside Diller's aggressive open-market accumulation.
insights.speaker: Aaron Cowen
insights.so_what: Aggressive share count reduction plus insider buying compounds per-share value of the hidden Asian/Dubai assets.
insights.quote: Rarely have I ever seen a company in 6 years buy half their float back.

insights.claim: David Friedberg argues Las Vegas casino properties are undermonetized on entertainment, citing a friend who says incremental EBITDA runs ~$1M/day whenever there's a show at the Sphere, implying Diller's entertainment-revamp thesis could lift gambling revenue materially.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Entertainment draw drives gambling volume, an additional upside lever beyond the Asia/Dubai assets even if those stall.
insights.quote: they're making a million bucks a day in incremental ibita every day that they have a show at the sphere

themes.theme: Talen Energy — AI power demand and sub-replacement-cost nuclear/gas

insights.claim: Talen Energy is pitched as a long: an EV of ~$25B versus ~$45B replacement cost, owning 2 GW nuclear and ~6 GW gas baseload, making the equity more than a double just to reach replacement value.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Hard-asset arbitrage: buy below replacement cost, sell at premium when the market wakes up (Sam Zell playbook).
insights.quote: you could purchase this company at a $25 billion enterprise value. the replacement cost is 45 billion.

insights.claim: The presenter argues power markets stay tight for 20 years even without AI; AI demand merely turbocharges an already-structural shortage caused by two decades of flat US demand and offshored power-hungry industry to China.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: De-risks the power thesis from AI-capex skepticism; even a robotics/AI slowdown leaves power scarce.
insights.quote: We do not need AI demand to keep the power markets incredibly tight for the next 20 years. AI demand just turbocharges.

insights.claim: China now has roughly 3x US power generation capacity, having started the cycle 20 years ago with half of US capacity, framing a 'copy China, build power' national mandate tied to AI, robotics, and military competitiveness.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: A structural, bipartisan buildout argument supporting sustained high power prices and IPP valuations.
insights.quote: we started out this cycle with having 2x the power generation that China had... today, China has three times the power generation capacity that we have.

insights.claim: The PJM grid operator forecasts ~106 GW of new power needed over 10 years in one region — roughly the size of Japan's total consumption — yet PJM power prices remain too low to stimulate new capacity.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Supply-demand imbalance implies coal retirements stall and power prices must rise, benefiting incumbent baseload owners.
insights.quote: the power price is still too low to stimulate new capacity. The math still doesn't work, which is really mindboggling.

insights.claim: The presenter frames a data center as economically identical to an oil refinery — $50B per GW capex turning electricity into 'tokens/intelligence' — making power the irreplaceable input commodity.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: A mental model for valuing power assets as the scarce feedstock of the AI economy rather than as utilities.
insights.quote: you put electricity in and on the other end instead of gasoline or jet fuel out comes photons or tokens or intelligence

insights.claim: Three-scenario Talen valuation: doing nothing yields ~$50/share FCF (~7x vs ~15x for US infra, a double); more contracts/higher power prices gets ~$70/share (~$1,050 implied); building ~4 GW of new capacity gets >$100/share FCF — versus a high-$300s stock.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Layered optionality with a hard floor: a double on inertia, larger upside on contracting and buildout.
insights.quote: these guys will be generating $50 a share of free cash flow per year... it's about seven times free cash flow.

insights.claim: Microsoft induced Constellation Energy to restart the Three Mile Island nuclear reactor by signing a 20-year PPA at ~$100/MWh (vs ~$50 grid price), illustrating hyperscalers locking in fixed-price power and bidding up scarce clean baseload.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Hyperscaler PPAs at 2x grid prices reset the value of existing nuclear capacity and signal premium pricing for Talen deals.
insights.quote: Power prices stay $50 a megawatt hour. We'll pay you a hundred a year for 20 years minimum price for you guys to start this up.

insights.claim: The thesis does not require behind-the-meter colocation to work; it is fundamentally a bet that clean firm baseload is scarce regardless of whether power flows in front of or behind the meter.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Insulates the trade from regulatory crackdowns on behind-the-meter colocation deals.

insights.claim: The presenter expects bridge power (fuel cells, Caterpillar/Solar gas turbines, eventually orbital compute) to be used regardless of high LCOE because a $50B data center cannot sit idle 3 years waiting for baseload CCGT.
insights.speaker: Daniel (Talen presenter)
insights.so_what: Bridge-power suppliers and turbine makers benefit from urgency; cost is no object during the buildout crunch.
insights.quote: you don't want it to sit idle for 3 years waiting for your base load CCGT so you do whatever it takes

insights.claim: Friedberg counters that Talen is more interest-rate sensitive than MGM because PPA cash flows are bond-like, so a rate spike would compress the assumed 15x multiple and Talen's full upside is ~8 years out.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Rate risk is the key downside variable for contracted power names; size accordingly versus shorter-duration ideas.
insights.quote: if interest rates shoot up which some might argue there's there's risk there you actually get margin compression from that 15x outlook

insights.claim: Both Friedberg and the presenter flag the main risk to Talen as exogenous government action — price caps, lab nationalization, or new laws on AI compute and utility supply — rather than anything company-specific.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Political/regulatory tail risk on AI power, not operations, is the thing to hedge or monitor for the trade.
insights.quote: the big negative externality for talent is nothing to do with talent... It's like something over the top from the US government caps prices

themes.theme: Actis Oncology — radiopharma with a China-proof moat

insights.claim: Oleg Nelman of EcoR1 Capital is long Actis Oncology (AKTS), a ~$1B market cap / ~$500M EV radiopharmaceutical company with 3+ years of cash, arguing it could be worth ~$10B or ~$200/share if even one program reaches market.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Large asymmetric biotech long with multiple shots on goal and near-term clinical catalysts.
insights.quote: We think Actis could be worth 10 billion or $200 per share if even one of their programs makes it to market.

insights.claim: Actis IPO'd at $300M, was 18x oversubscribed, and Eli Lilly backstopped it with a $100M order; Lilly, Bristol, Novartis, and Bayer are all building radiotherapy capability amid ~$15B of recent M&A in the space.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Deep-pocketed strategic buyers and a hot M&A backdrop create a credible takeout floor on positive data.
insights.quote: There's been 15 billion in M&A and dealmaking in radiotherapy in the last few years and we're very much in the early innings.

insights.claim: Actis targets validated antigens — Nectin-4 (bladder cancer) and B7-H3 (expressed on prostate, colorectal, and lung, the big three solid tumors) — with initial clinical data guided for 2027, Nectin-4 as early as Q1.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Defined near-term binary catalysts; a positive readout could re-rate the whole mini-protein platform, not just one program.
insights.quote: If either program shows a signal, the company is likely to get value not only for those programs but the entire mini protein platform.

insights.claim: Nelman argues radiopharma has a rare biotech moat because generics don't traffic in radioisotopes and the actinium payload (made from radium-233, a US 1950s-60s nuclear waste product) is effectively off-limits to China.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Supply-chain moat differentiates this from broadly China-replicable biologics, though challenged by panelists.
insights.quote: because the class involves radioisotopes it's off limits to China. So unlike most of biotech there's a real moat

insights.claim: Nelman contends GLP-1 / obesity drugs are among the best longevity drugs available because they mimic caloric restriction — the only intervention with hard data showing life extension — and normalize injectable preventive pharma.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Reframes GLP-1 demand as a durable longevity/healthspan category, supporting the long-term obesity-drug TAM.
insights.quote: one of the only things that's ever been shown... to extend life is caloric restriction and that's literally what all the obesity drugs do.

insights.claim: Nelman attributes Actis trading flat since IPO to biotech investors being extremely short-term, expecting accumulation to begin in 2H ahead of Q1 2027 data — a recurring depressed-biotech dynamic.
insights.speaker: Oleg Nelman
insights.so_what: Timing edge: a window to accumulate before generalist/specialist flows arrive ahead of the data catalyst.
insights.quote: biotech investors are so insanely short-term oriented that even though we're now call it eight or nine months from data that's still way too long

insights.claim: Friedberg, an Actis bull on the platform upside, flags real downside: he is invested in a competing D-protein conjugate showing strong solid-tumor efficacy, plus China replication risk, putting Actis downside at ~50-75% on a bad readout or new modality.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: Competitive modality risk and China copying temper the moat thesis; size as a lottery ticket, not a core position.
insights.quote: the downside's probably 50 75% if they get a bad readout or China or some new modality comes out.

themes.theme: GeoNet (GEOD) — decentralized RTK network as physical-AI infrastructure

insights.claim: Kyle Samani (founder of Multicoin Capital) is long the GeoNet token (GEOD), a Solana-based decentralized RTK geolocation network providing ~2cm precision (100x GPS), pitched as the rails for physical AI and robotics.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: A crypto play on the robotics/physical-AI tailwind with real revenue and a buyback mechanism.
insights.quote: Geonet... uses a technology called RTK or real-time kinematics where you can localize your location down to about 2 centimeters

insights.claim: GeoNet has ~22,000 base stations (roughly 2x the combined ~12,000 of incumbents Trimble, Hexagon, Topcon built over 20-30 years), live in 150 countries and covering ~80% of global population, via a decentralized model where hobbyists buy ~$few-hundred stations and earn GEOD tokens.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Token incentives bootstrapped a network faster and cheaper than incumbents, implying a structural cost advantage.
insights.quote: all of them combined have roughly 12,000 base stations... today they are roughly twice the size of the next three guys combined.

insights.claim: GeoNet just crossed ~$11M annualized revenue growing >3x YoY, with customers including John Deere (GUS spraying), DJI, TomTom, and robotic-mower makers; the USDA subsidizes farmers to adopt RTK precision ag largely powered by GeoNet.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Real blue-chip customer revenue distinguishes GeoNet from failed token-reward DePIN projects.
insights.quote: the business just crossed about $1 million in annualized run rate a few days ago. Uh, and it's growing more than 3x year-over-year.

insights.claim: GeoNet uses ~80% of revenue (~$8.8M/yr) to make verifiable open-market GEOD token buybacks on Solana, with the remaining 20% funding R&D and BD — making the token effectively a revenue-share/securitized cash-flow claim.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Direct buyback linkage means token value tracks customer revenue, but also raises securities-law questions under the Clarity Act.
insights.quote: 80% of it is being used to make open market purchases of Geo tokens... that means $8.8 million right now per year is going into buying

insights.claim: GeoNet trades at ~$150M fully diluted (~$60-70M reported on coin sites due to unlocked float) growing >3x/year, which Samani calls extremely cheap, attributing the discount to the current crypto bear market.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: If revenue triples as guided, the implied multiple looks low; bear-market neglect is the entry-point thesis.
insights.quote: It's trading at $150 million valuation and it's going to grow 3x this year. It's an unbelievably cheap asset.

insights.claim: GeoNet customer cohorts ramp from ~$60K/year in year one to ~$170K/year in year two (~3x), and net new customers grew 5x last year, supporting a path to >3x revenue this year.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Strong net revenue retention plus customer growth underpins the tripling guidance and network-effect monopoly claim.
insights.quote: After two years though, they're usually spending about $170,000 per year. So the average Geonet customer is growing their their revenue with GeoNet about 3x in that second year.

insights.claim: Samani argues ground-based RTK beats LEO-satellite GPS alternatives on both cost (base stations are a few hundred dollars vs satellite launch) and energy use, the latter being decisive for battery-sensitive drones, even if satellites eventually challenge GPS.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the satellite-disruption bear case; energy efficiency is the durable edge for mobile/robotics use.
insights.quote: Going to space just consumes way more energy than going to a base station that's you know on the ground.

insights.claim: John Deere and others would switch from owning their own RTK capex networks to GeoNet because it is roughly a third to a quarter of the cost (opex vs capex) and available everywhere, shortening their equipment sales cycle.
insights.speaker: Kyle Samani
insights.so_what: Cost displacement of incumbent RTK is the adoption driver; watch whether OEMs actually standardize on GeoNet.
insights.quote: GeoNet is is probably a third to half sorry a third to a quarter the price um than buying up your own capex and doing it.

themes.theme: Portfolio construction, sizing and judge verdicts

insights.claim: Chamath bucketed the four ideas by sizing: MGM and Talen can absorb tens of millions with downside protection, while Actis and GeoNet are lottery-ticket positions — GeoNet so illiquid he says even $1M would move the market, so he'd start at $10-30K.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: Liquidity, not just expected return, dictates position sizing across asymmetric ideas — a practical allocation lens.
insights.quote: talent. I think they could absorb tens of millions and people wouldn't bite an eyelash... I love Kyle's idea. The problem is I I could not get enough working for me

insights.claim: Friedberg ranked the ideas MGM > Talen > Actis > GeoNet, citing MGM's best risk/reward with a Diller-bid floor and ~3x in two years even if the bid is rejected.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: A generalist's ranking favoring downside-protected catalysts over high-variance lottery tickets.
insights.quote: I do like the floor on the bid and then you got call it 3x in 2 years even if this bid goes nowhere

insights.claim: Friedberg believes LEO satellites will replace all RTK and GPS within the next decade as an inevitable piggyback on already-deploying constellations, the basis for ranking GeoNet last.
insights.speaker: David Friedberg
insights.so_what: A direct bear case on GeoNet's terminal value; the satellite-vs-ground-station debate is the key swing factor.
insights.quote: I just think the space thing is is likely the path. It's going to replace all RTK and all GPS in the next decade.

insights.claim: MGM (Aaron Cowen) won both the live bestie panel vote and, in an upset flipping the audience result, the overall — the audience had ranked Talen first (50%), MGM second (24%), Actis third (21%), GeoNet last (5%).
insights.speaker: host/narrator
insights.so_what: Signals where a sophisticated audience and panel see best risk/reward; MGM and Talen are the consensus core picks.
insights.quote: First place Aaron Cowen. Big upset. Flip the audience vote.

insights.claim: Chamath endorses the Stan Druckenmiller principle that conviction requires skin in the game, and Chamath says he put $200K into each of MGM and Talen in real time during the event.
insights.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
insights.so_what: A live revealed-preference signal of which pitches the panel actually backed with capital.
insights.quote: I apply the Stan Ducken Miller school of invest... if you don't have any skin in the game, you don't care.
